Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 65 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

So clicking on the orange object at (12, 24) caused:

- Orange moved from (12, 24) to (13, 16) (a new position)
This is a swap! The white diamond and orange object swapped positions. The orange object moved to where the white diamond was, and the white diamond moved to where the orange was.
Wait, but the orange object moved to (13, 16), not (9, 49) where the white diamond was. So it's not a simple swap.
Hmm, let me re-examine. The white diamond was at (9, 49) and the orange was at (12, 24). After clicking on the orange:
- White diamond is now at (12, 24) (orange's old position)
- Orange is now at (13, 16) (not the white diamond's old position)
So the orange moved to (13, 16), which is different from (9, 49). This is strange.

Actually, I think I need to reconsider. The white diamond at (9, 49) and the orange at (12, 24) are connected by a white line. When I clicked on the orange, the white diamond moved to the orange's position, and the orange moved to... somewhere else.
Let me check the distance between positions:

The orange moved from (12, 24) to (13, 16). The G object is at (6, 17). The distance from (13, 16) to (6, 17) is 7 rows and 1 column. The distance from (12, 24) to (9, 49) is 3 rows and 25 columns.
Hmm, this doesn't seem to follow a simple pattern. Let me think about this differently.

With the white diamond W(9,49) somewhere in the middle.
When I clicked on the orange at (12, 24), the white diamond moved to (12, 24) and the orange moved to (13, 16). The position (13, 16) is near the G at (6, 17).
Actually, wait. Let me check if (13, 16) is where the white diamond was BEFORE the first action. In the very first state, the white diamond was at (6, 17). After the first action (clicking on (9, 49)), the white diamond moved to (9, 49) and the G moved to (6, 17).

Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try clicking on the orange object at (13, 16) to see what happens.
